era_context: The year 1358 falls in the mid-14th century, a period of significant political and military upheaval across Eurasia. The Hundred Years' War between England and France was in a phase of English military dominance, following the English victory at Poitiers (1356). The Ottoman Empire was consolidating power in Anatolia and beginning its expansion into the Balkans. In China, the Yuan Dynasty was in its final decades, with regional warlords competing for supremacy. The Delhi Sultanate controlled much of India. This was an era of demographic recovery from the Black Death, military innovation, and the early stirrings of the Renaissance.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesoamerica and South America (pre-Columbian records fragmentary)
Central Asia (limited sources beyond Ottoman and Chinese perspectives)

events.title: Jacquerie (Peasant Revolt) in France
events.description: The Jacquerie, a major peasant uprising in northern France, erupts in May 1358 in response to heavy taxation, military devastation from the Hundred Years' War, and feudal oppression. The revolt is brutally suppressed by the nobility within weeks, but it reflects widespread social discontent across Europe in the post-plague era, when labor scarcity had emboldened the lower classes to demand better conditions. The Jacquerie is one of the most significant peasant uprisings of the medieval period and presages later social unrest, including the English Peasants' Revolt of 1381.
events.certainty_note: The Jacquerie is one of the most thoroughly documented peasant uprisings of the medieval period. The date (May 1358) and key events are secure.

events.title: Étienne Marcel leads Paris uprising
events.description: Étienne Marcel, a wealthy Paris merchant and provost of the merchants, leads an uprising in Paris in 1358 in response to the political chaos and military failures of the Hundred Years' War. Marcel attempts to reform the French government and reduce the power of the nobility. His uprising is eventually suppressed, and Marcel is killed in 1358. The uprising reflects the broader social and political instability of the period and the emergence of urban merchant classes as political actors.
key_figures: Dauphin Charles (future Charles V, opponent of Marcel)
events.certainty_note: Étienne Marcel's uprising and death in 1358 are well-documented in contemporary chronicles and municipal records. The date is secure.

events.title: Zhu Yuanzhang consolidates power in southern China
events.description: Zhu Yuanzhang, the future Hongwu Emperor, continues his military campaigns to consolidate control over southern China. By 1358, he has eliminated several major rivals and is the dominant power in the Yangtze River valley. His rise to power is part of the broader collapse of the Yuan Dynasty and the emergence of the Ming Dynasty, which will rule China from 1368 to 1644.
events.certainty_note: Zhu Yuanzhang's military campaigns in the 1350s are well-documented in Chinese historical records, but specific events for 1358 are not always precisely dated.

events.title: Ottoman Empire consolidates Anatolian territories
events.description: The Ottoman Empire, under Orhan I (r. 1323–1362) or his successor Murad I, continues to consolidate control over Anatolian territories and begins serious military incursions into the Balkans. By 1358, Ottoman forces are a major regional power, threatening Byzantine territories and absorbing smaller beylik states. This consolidation is part of a broader Ottoman strategy to become a major imperial power.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion in the late 1350s is attested in both Ottoman and Byzantine sources, but specific events for 1358 are not always precisely dated. The broader trajectory is secure.

disconfirming_evidence: The Jacquerie and Étienne Marcel's uprising are sometimes conflated or confused in secondary sources; they were contemporary but distinct events. The Jacquerie was primarily a rural peasant revolt, while Marcel's uprising was an urban merchant-led movement. Some sources place Marcel's death in 1358, others in 1359; the exact date is debated. Zhu Yuanzhang's specific military movements in 1358 are difficult to pinpoint to a single year, as campaigns often spanned multiple years and sources sometimes compress or expand timelines.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1358 is strong for Western Europe (the Jacquerie and Étienne Marcel's uprising are exhaustively documented in contemporary chronicles and municipal records). Chinese sources are substantial but often compiled after the fact. Ottoman sources are less precise for this specific year. The Jacquerie and Marcel's uprising are significant events in European social and political history, yet they are often overshadowed in popular historiography by military events like the Hundred Years' War. Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Americas remain largely absent from surviving written records.

graph_edges.note: Part of broader 14th-century peasant unrest across Europe
Urban and rural uprisings occur simultaneously in France
Zhu Yuanzhang's consolidation leads to Battle of Poyang Lake
